content:
Hello there

Thunderbird stopped working. With the message : message, "can't load xpcom" This message is there because files are corrupted, or missing or mismatched following after a mismatched or failed update.


Deleting all folder from thunderbird Make an backup from the profile and be sure it s safe. Be sure that Thunderbird is deleted and all is deleted from the system if possible use a software cleaner. Use a new the latest thunderbird installer. https://www.thunderbird.net/en-US/thunderbird/all/ Be sure that you do not simply overwrite the old version on the desktop.

Disabling the antivirus In the meanwhile disable the anti virus and firewalls and Vpn service for a good moment. To be sure that these services aren t blocking something. Also antivirus can block certain files after updates.

Renaming folders If the reinstalled aren t working try to rename the existing Thunderbird map into oldThunderbird map and try to reinstall again.

You cant reinstall the new version because all of the software has to be deleted from the desktop and system. Try to use the software cleaner like cc cleaner from google.

content:
All mail account (email address) passwords can only be created or modified via the relevant webmail account which you access directly via a browser. If you have forgotten a password for a mail account/email address then you will find a 'forgotten password' option or similar when attempting to logon to the webmail account - you need to use it and follow instructions to regain access.

Once a password has been reset via webmail account, you can then update the stored password in Thunderbird:

  • Settings > Privacy & Security
  • clickon 'Saved Passwords'
  • clickon 'Show Passwords'
  • Right clickon all relevant lines and select 'Edit Password'
  • clear all content, enter new password and click on 'Close'
  • Then Exit Thunderbird, wait a few moments and restart Thunderbird.

Thunderbird option to set a Primary Password Thunderbird does have the option to create a Primary Password. It's sole purpose is to prevent prying eyes from accessing and seeing the stored passwords. It does not prevent any access to viewing emails.

  • https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/protect-your-thunderbird-passwords-primary-password

Help Articles: OpenPGP in Thunderbird - HOWTO and FAQ

  • https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/openpgp-thunderbird-howto-and-faq

Setup your email account for using End-To-End Encryption

  • https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/thunderbird-help-setup-account-e2ee

Email End to End Encryption

  • Settings > Privacy & Security
  • Scroll down to the bottom to find section : 'Email End-to-End Encryption'
  • Options to enable or auto disable are set in this location.

content:
What search are you using - 'Quick Filter', 'Global Search' or via 'Search Messages' ?

IF 'Lennart' is a 'Display Name' : Do any of the emails received this year actually contain that Display Name with the email address ? In other words did the sender include Lennart as display name? If no then it would explain why Lennart fails but email address works.

How to check :

  • Select email so it displays in Message Pane
  • click on 'More' and select 'View Source'

It opens in a new window. You would need to scroll down past most of the headers to locate the 'From' to see if those emails since this year do contain that display name.


If you used 'Global Search': The time period can be selected - did you select this year ? Is 2026 shown or any dates across the top ? Use the 'Toggle Timeline' to enable if not visible.

Have you by any chance selected another filter which may restrict on eg: a folder?

content:
Thunderbird does not reject passwords that will be the server you are attempting to connect to which is objecting to something being wrong.

It could be the password is wrong or password is correct but not for the other server settings. More info is needed.

What version of Thunderbird are you using ? What server are you trying to get a connection ? gmail ? yahoo ? aol ? etc What server settings are you using ? You could simply upload images that show Account Settings > Server Settings and just scrub out the email address/username.

It would also be helpful if you could upload an image of the error message.

In the meantime please check to see if you have recently enabled a VPN. Some Anti-Virus now provide a VPN and in several cases I've come across people who did not realise it was there let alone enabled. Please switch it off as many server do not like it as it hides your real IP address and server may think you are a hacker - so it blocks access.

Firewall Access Firewall which may the defaul on computer or one controlled by an Anti-Virus. Thunderbird program must be set up as an allowed program to ensure nothing gets blocked.

content: A simple approach is to 1) increase the size of the cache so that compacting is less frequent, and 2) when the prompt appears, wait until the mailboxes update.
creator: davidsk

content:
Thank you for your reply. However, it does not address the root cause of the problem. Your first suggestion will indeed cause the compacting to occur less frequently, but logically cause the time to compact to increase with the increase in cache size. Option 2 doesn't really help since simply waiting for all of the mailboxes to update while the interface is inaccessible while the compact window has the focus doesn't allow truly knowing when that is done. I have waited what seemed to be enough time (several minutes) only to be told that compacting can't be completed because the a mailbox is still updating. The suggested approaches have been done but the root cause or the problem isn't resolved.

So, the question I originally asked concerns user control over the timing of the mailbox compacting. So to restate: is there a way for the user to delay the compacting process, or have Thunderbird not initiate it until it is safe to do so to begin with?
creator: palotta

content:
PAlotta said
It would seem like a simple matter to either give the user a way to delay the compacting process, or have Thunderbird not initiate it until it is safe to do so to begin with.

Decent ideas, for which I have filed bug reports. But so far no bites.

You're seeing two different issues, one of which is the potential compact savings is incorrect, resulting in constant compacts on startup. This issue is rare, but is real and has resisted definition to the point of being able to fix it.

You could increase the value as Rick suggests but inevitable compacts will again happen on startup. And so, unfortunately the only way to avoid this is to disable automatic compact, and periodically do the maintenance compacts manually yourself.
